name: Benzaiten
description: Benzaiten, also known as Benten, is a Japanese Buddhist goddess who originated from the Hindu goddess Saraswati. She is one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin) in Japanese folklore, representing wealth, music, eloquence, and water. Often depicted playing a biwa (Japanese lute), she is associated with rivers, lakes, and the sea. Benzaiten's influence extends to good fortune, victory in war, and academic success. Temples dedicated to her are frequently found near bodies of water, reflecting her connection to fluidity and adaptability. She is revered for her grace, beauty, and the power she holds over artistic expression and financial prosperity. While originating from a Hindu deity, Benzaiten has been thoroughly integrated into Japanese culture, evolving into a unique figure with specific Japanese attributes and iconography. Her worship provides blessings related to creative pursuits, financial stability, and overall good fortune.
subtitle: Goddess of Music, Wealth, and Eloquence

keyMyths.title: Benzaiten and the Dragon King
keyMyths.description: Benzaiten calmed the wrath of a dragon king, saving the people from his destructive power. In return, the dragon king offered her a wish-granting jewel.

keyMyths.title: Benzaiten's Musical Prowess
keyMyths.description: Benzaiten's music is said to be so beautiful and enchanting that it can calm even the most savage beasts. Her biwa playing brings peace and harmony.

keyMyths.title: Benzaiten and Uga Benzaiten
keyMyths.description: Sometimes Benzaiten is depicted with a snake or dragon head (Uga Benzaiten), representing a more powerful and fearsome aspect of the goddess.

sources.author: Helen Hardacre
sources.work: Marketing the Menacing Fudo: New Religions and the Commodification of Buddhist Protection
sources.description: Discusses Benzaiten's role in Japanese religious practice.
sources.text: Discusses Benzaiten's role in Japanese religious practice.

sources.author: Kiyota Jakuun
sources.description: Details the iconography and placement of Benzaiten statues.
